moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the flexible Nf3, which is the hallmark of the King's Indian Attack system. This move controls e5 and d4—two central squares—without immediately committing our pawn structure, allowing us to adapt to Black's setup. The KIA is unique because it doesn't fight for the center directly at first; instead, we'll fianchetto our kingside bishop and build a kingside pressure while keeping our position solid.

Black immediately stakes a claim in the center with d5, challenging us to decide how we'll respond. This is actually a critical moment: Black could have played d6 or c5, but the aggressive d5 tells us Black wants to control space early.

We commit to the fianchetto setup, preparing to place our bishop on g2 where it will dominate the long diagonal (a1-h8) and exert long-range pressure on Black's center and queenside. The g3 move also prepares to castle kingside safely while maintaining flexibility—we haven't yet committed our central pawns, so we can respond naturally to Black's setup.

Black pushes for space and control, but this aggressive pawn advance actually plays into the KIA's hands. By accepting this central tension, Black has committed pawns to e5 and d5, which can become targets or restrict their own pieces later. We can now proceed with Bg2 and castling, building our position methodically while Black's central pawns may become vulnerable to maneuvering tactics.
